function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Meta's FY2025 10-K discloses consolidated revenue and average MAUs across its Family of Apps. Using Meta's disclosed FY2025 revenue and MAU figures, calculate Meta's implied annual revenue per MAU. Perform the same calculation for SpaceX's AI segment using disclosed FY2025 AI segment revenue and the 550 million combined MAU figure as of March 31, 2026. Express the ratio between the two figures. What does the gap imply about the relative stage of monetization maturity for the X-plus-Grok platform versus a fully mature social advertising business? Identify two structural reasons — one related to revenue mix and one related to the filing's disclosure design — why this ratio cannot be interpreted as a straightforward monetization efficiency comparison.
I'll start by gathering the necessary data from both Meta's FY2025 10-K and SpaceX's S-1 filing. Let me search for both simultaneously.

5. Express the ratio between the two figures
6. Analyze what the gap implies about monetization maturity
7. Identify two structural reasons (one revenue mix, one disclosure design) why this ratio can't be a straightforward comparison

Based on the provided document, the AI segment revenue for FY2025 (the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025) was **$3,201 million**.

Based on the provided text, the **550 million MAU (Monthly Active Users)** figure represents the **combined, deduplicated user base across the integrated Grok and X platforms** for the 30-day period ending March 31, 2026. 

Here are the specific details and context surrounding this metric:

*   **Definition & Methodology:** An MAU is defined as a registered user who interacted with Grok or X via web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day measurement period. The company uses sign-in traffic to deduplicate the data, ensuring that users who access *both* platforms are only counted once.
*   **Growth:** The 550 million figure represents an increase from approximately 520 million MAUs as of December 31, 2025. It is part of a broader ecosystem of over 1.3 billion supported accounts that were active in the trailing twelve months.
*   **Grok AI Adoption:** Out of the 550 million combined MAUs, approximately **117 million MAUs** specifically utilized Grok’s AI features.
*   **Platform Engagement:** This combined user base generated approximately 350 million daily posts.
*   **Business Strategy Context:** While the 550 million MAU figure demonstrates massive scale and engagement, the company explicitly notes that it manages its business with the primary objective of driving sustainable revenue growth and profitability (through advertising and paid subscriptions) rather than focusing solely on growing or maintaining MAU levels.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, Meta **does not disclose** Monthly Active People (MAP) or Monthly Active Users (MAU) metrics. 

Instead, the document explicitly states that the company's key operational "Family metrics" are **Daily Active People (DAP)** and **Average Revenue Per Person (ARPP)**. 

The only reference to "monthly" in the text relates to the methodology used to calculate DAP, noting that the machine learning models are calibrated against user surveys where "questions are based on monthly usage."

For context on user engagement, the text provides the following **Daily Active People (DAP)** metrics for the Family of Apps:
* **December 2025:** 3.58 billion (on average), a 7% increase year-over-year.
* **December 2024:** 3.35 billion (on average).

Based on the provided text, here are the user, engagement, and "Family" metrics disclosed for the Family of Apps in 2025. 

### 1. Daily Active People (DAP)
* **Definition:** A registered and logged-in user of Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and/or WhatsApp who visited at least one of these products via a mobile app or web/mobile browser on a given day. The company uses machine learning and data signals to deduplicate accounts across apps and count unique individuals.
* **2025 Data:** Worldwide DAP averaged **3.58 billion** in December 2025, representing a **7% year-over-year increase** (up from 3.35 billion in December 2024).
* **Limitations:** The company notes an estimated error margin of approximately **3%** for worldwide DAP due to the complexities of attributing multiple accounts to single individuals across encrypted and non-encrypted apps.

### 2. Violating Accounts
* **Definition:** Accounts believed to be intended for purposes that violate terms of service, such as bots and spam. 
* **2025 Data:** In the fourth quarter of 2025, Meta estimated that **less than 5%** of its worldwide DAP consisted solely of violating accounts (an increase from prior estimations due to methodology updates and improved data signals).

### 3. Average Revenue Per Person (ARPP)
* *Note: While technically a monetization metric, the text explicitly groups ARPP with DAP under the umbrella of "Family metrics."*
* **Definition:** Family of Apps (FoA) revenue during a given quarter, divided by the average number of DAP at the beginning and end of the quarter.
* **2025 Data:** The text provides a quarterly data table for ARPP (values include $12.33, $11.20, $11.89, $12.29, $14.25, $12.36, $13.65, $14.46, and $16.56). 

### 4. Advertising Engagement Metrics
While not strictly "user base" metrics, these engagement-driven advertising metrics are tied to user activity:
* **Ad Impressions:** Increased by **12% year-over-year** in 2025 across the Family of Apps.
* **Average Price Per Ad:** Increased by **9% year-over-year** in 2025.

---

### ⚠️ Note on MAP, MAU, and Monthly Active People
**Monthly Active People (MAP)**, **Monthly Active Users (MAU)**, and **Monthly Active People** are **NOT disclosed** as reported metrics in the provided text. 
* The text only references "monthly usage" in the context of *user surveys* used to calibrate the machine learning models that estimate DAP. Meta has shifted its primary public reporting focus entirely to DAP (Daily Active People) to measure community size and engagement.

arguments.prompt: What are the quarterly ARPP (Average Revenue Per Person) figures for FY2025 in {{meta_10k_2025}}? List all quarterly values and which quarters they correspond to.
What are the quarterly DAP (Daily Active People) figures for each quarter of 2025 in {{meta_10k_2025}}? List the DAP for March, June, September, and December 2025.

What is the SpaceX AI segment revenue breakdown and details for FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}? Include any details about the AI segment's revenue sources, advertising revenue, subscription revenue, and total revenue.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, the quarterly Average Revenue Per Person (ARPP) figures for FY2025 are as follows:

* **Q1 2025:** $12.36
* **Q2 2025:** $13.65
* **Q3 2025:** $14.46
* **Q4 2025:** $16.56

*(Note: The sum of these four quarters equals the reported annual worldwide ARPP for 2025 of $57.03. The preceding values in the data table correspond to prior quarters in 2024 and late 2023).*

Based on the provided text, the specific quarterly Daily Active People (DAP) figures for March, June, and September 2025 are **not mentioned**. 

The only DAP figure provided in the text is for the end of the year:
* **December 2025:** 3.58 billion (an increase of 7% year-over-year from 3.35 billion in December 2024).

Based on the provided financial documents, here is the detailed breakdown and analysis of the **AI Segment's revenue and financial performance for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025 (FY2025)**. 

*(Note: The provided text outlines a consolidated entity comprising Space, Connectivity, and AI segments, where the AI segment primarily encompasses the operations of the X platform and Grok AI).*

### 1. Total AI Segment Revenue & Breakdown (FY2025)
For the full year 2025, the AI segment generated **$3,201 million** in total revenue. The revenue is disaggregated into two primary categories:
*   **Advertising:** $1,844 million
*   **AI Solutions & Infrastructure:** $1,357 million

### 2. Revenue Sources & Recognition Policies
The AI segment generates revenue from digital platform services offered to both consumers and enterprise customers. 
*   **Advertising Revenue:** 
    *   **Source:** Sale of ad products displayed on the X platform.
    *   **Recognition:** Recognized in the period the ad is delivered, evidenced by user engagement satisfying the advertiser's selected criteria. 
    *   **Terms & Seasonality:** Contracts are typically cancellable, short-term arrangements. Revenue experiences seasonality, peaking in Q4 (due to end-of-year advertiser spending) and hitting its lowest point in Q1.
*   **AI Solutions & Infrastructure Revenue:**
    *   **Sources:** Includes premium subscriptions (X and Grok), data licensing arrangements, and API services.
    *   **Recognition (Subscriptions):** Recognized ratably over the subscription term, which ranges from month-to-month up to one year.
    *   **Recognition (Data Licensing):** Recognized ratably over the contract period, which ranges from month-to-month up to two years.

### 3. Year-over-Year Growth Drivers (2025 vs. 2024)
Total AI segment revenue increased by **$581 million (or 22.2%)** compared to FY2024 ($2,620 million). This growth was driven by:
*   **Advertising (+$116 million):** Growth was fueled by increased advertising spend from partners on the X platform.
*   **AI Solutions & Infrastructure (+$465 million):** This surge was primarily attributed to:
    *   A **$365 million** increase in X and Grok premium subscription revenue.
    *   An **$88 million** increase in revenue from data licensing arrangements.

### 4. Segment Profitability & Key Expenses (FY2025)
The AI segment is in an earlier stage of development regarding its compute infrastructure, resulting in heavy investments and operating losses for the fiscal year:
*   **Loss from Operations:** $(6,355) million
*   **Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** $(1,237) million
*   **Capital Expenditures (CapEx):** $12,727 million (reflecting massive investments in AI data centers and compute infrastructure).

**Key Expense Drivers in FY2025:**
*   **Cost of Revenue:** Increased by $491 million (29.1%) YoY. This was primarily driven by higher infrastructure and cloud computing costs ($412 million) to support subscriber growth, higher employee compensation ($90 million), higher revenue share and content creator fees ($45 million), and increased payment processor fees ($28 million).
*   **Research & Development (R&D):** R&D expenses saw a massive increase (contributing to a $3,888 million YoY jump in segment R&D costs) driven by the depreciation of GPU hardware and cloud computing costs associated with rapid AI data center expansions. 
*   **Restructuring Charges:** The segment incurred $487 million in restructuring charges during the year.

arguments.prompt: How is the AI segment defined in {{spacex_s1}}? What businesses, products, and revenue streams are included in the AI segment? How are inter-segment revenues or shared costs handled?

What is the revenue mix breakdown for the AI segment in {{spacex_s1}}? What percentage is advertising vs subscriptions vs data licensing vs API? Are there any inter-segment eliminations or shared revenue with other segments?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here is how the AI segment is defined, including its businesses, products, revenue streams, and the handling of shared costs:

### **Businesses and Products in the AI Segment**
The AI segment was formed through the acquisition of **xAI** in February 2026. It operates as a highly vertically integrated AI platform that includes:
*   **Grok:** The company's "truth-seeking" frontier AI model.
*   **X:** The real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform.
*   **AI Compute Infrastructure:** Gigawatt-scale terrestrial and planned orbital data centers designed to train models and provide inference.
*   **AI Solutions:** Products and services offered to both consumer and enterprise customers. 

### **Revenue Streams**
The AI segment generates revenue primarily from the sale of digital platform services, which are broken down into the following streams:
*   **Advertising:** Revenue from ad products displayed on the X platform (recognized when a user engages with the ad based on the advertiser's criteria).
*   **AI Solutions & Infrastructure:**
    *   **Subscriptions:** Premium subscriptions for X and Grok (ranging from month-to-month to one-year terms).
    *   **Data Licensing:** Arrangements where customers consume and benefit from licensed data (recognized ratably over periods up to two years).
    *   **API Access:** Providing access to Grok models for developers and enterprises (recognized ratably for stand-ready access or as services are consumed for usage-based arrangements).

### **Inter-Segment Revenues and Shared Costs**
The provided text **does not explicitly detail** the accounting policies for inter-segment revenue eliminations or how costs are shared *between* the three main reportable segments (Space, Connectivity, and AI). 

However, the text does provide a few details regarding internal cost allocations and overhead handling *within* the AI segment's financial reporting:
*   **Allocated Overhead:** The AI segment’s Cost of Revenue includes "allocated labor and overhead costs."
*   **Unallocated Overhead:** The AI segment’s Selling, General, and Administrative (SG&A) expenses include "facilities costs and other supporting overhead costs that are **not allocated to other departments**."
*   *(Note: The text mentions the "allocation of shared costs" regarding labor and materials, but this is specifically tied to estimating total costs for **Starshield** satellite production in the Space/Connectivity segments, not cross-segment AI sharing).*

Based on the provided text, here is the breakdown of the AI segment's revenue mix and the details regarding inter-segment activities:

### 1. Revenue Mix Breakdown: Advertising vs. Subscriptions vs. Data Licensing vs. API
The provided text **does not disclose the exact percentage or total dollar breakdown** between the individual sub-components of subscriptions, data licensing, and API. Instead, the company groups these three categories under a single reporting line item called **"AI Solutions & Infrastructure."** 

However, the text does provide the top-level split between **Advertising** and **AI Solutions & Infrastructure**, as well as the year-over-year *growth drivers* for the sub-categories.

**Top-Level AI Segment Revenue Split:**
Based on the disaggregated revenue tables, the mix between Advertising and AI Solutions & Infrastructure is as follows:

| Period | Advertising (% of AI Rev) | AI Solutions & Infrastructure (% of AI Rev) | Total AI Revenue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Q1 2026** | $343M **(41.9%)** | $475M **(58.1%)** | $818M |
| **FY 2025** | $1,844M **(57.6%)** | $1,357M **(42.4%)** | $3,201M |
| **FY 2024** | $1,728M **(66.0%)** | $892M **(34.0%)** | $2,620M |
| **FY 2023** | $2,323M **(78.5%)** | $638M **(21.5%)** | $2,961M |

*Note: The data shows a clear strategic shift, with AI Solutions & Infrastructure overtaking Advertising as the primary revenue driver for the AI segment by Q1 2026.*

**Insights into Sub-Categories (Subscriptions, Licensing, API):**
While exact total balances are not provided, the MD&A section reveals the dollar *increases* for specific sub-categories year-over-year, showing that Subscriptions and Data Licensing make up the vast majority of the "AI Solutions & Infrastructure" growth:
*   **FY 2025:** AI Solutions & Infra grew by $465M. This was driven by a **$365M increase in X and Grok subscriptions** and an **$88M increase in data licensing** (accounting for $453M of the growth, leaving roughly $12M attributable to API/other).
*   **FY 2024:** AI Solutions & Infra grew by $254M. This was driven by a **$157M increase in X subscriptions** and a **$90M increase in data licensing** (accounting for $247M of the growth).
*   **Q1 2026:** AI Solutions & Infra grew by $191M. This was driven by a **$177M increase in Grok and X subscriptions** and a **$12M increase in data licensing**.
*   **API Revenue:** The text outlines the accounting policy for Grok API access (recognized ratably or as consumed) but **never quantifies** the API revenue in dollars or percentages.

### 2. Inter-Segment Eliminations and Shared Revenue
**There are no inter-segment revenue eliminations or shared revenue arrangements mentioned for the AI segment.** All AI revenues (Advertising, Subscriptions, Data Licensing, and API) are generated from external consumers and enterprise customers.

However, the text does note an operational overlap regarding **internal launch capacity** between the segments:
*   The **Space segment** allocates launch capacity internally to the Connectivity segment and expects to *"allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future"* (likely for AI data center/satellite infrastructure buildouts). 
*   To avoid the need for inter-segment revenue eliminations, the company explicitly states: *"Our Space segment revenue only reflects our customer launches and customer activities."* 
*   This means that when the Space segment launches payloads for the AI or Connectivity segments, it is treated as an internal cost/transfer rather than recognized as Space segment revenue. Therefore, no inter-segment revenue is recorded or eliminated on the consolidated income statement for these activities.

steps.message: Now let me retrieve one more piece of information about the temporal alignment and disclosure design.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the data I need. Let me also check if there's any information about how the MAU figure relates to the AI segment specifically, and whether the 550 million MAU figure is appropriate to use against the full AI segment revenue.

Let me also check if there's any disclosure about the AI segment's revenue being generated from users who are not MAUs (e.g., enterprise API customers, data licensing customers).

Let me now compile my analysis:

**Meta FY2025:**
- Revenue: $200,966 million
- DAP (December 2025): 3.58 billion (Meta doesn't report MAU; DAP is the closest equivalent)
- Annual ARPP (disclosed): $57.03
- Calculated: $200,966M / 3,580M = $56.14 per DAP

**SpaceX AI Segment FY2025:**
- Revenue: $3,201 million
- MAU (March 31, 2026): 550 million
- Calculated: $3,201M / 550M = $5.82 per MAU

**Ratio:**
- Using direct calculation: $56.14 / $5.82 = 9.65x
- Using Meta's disclosed ARPP: $57.03 / $5.82 = 9.80x

Now for the structural reasons why this ratio can't be a straightforward comparison:

**1. Revenue Mix (structural reason):**
Meta's revenue is almost entirely advertising-driven, where every user is a potential ad impression. The AI segment's revenue is a mix of advertising ($1,844M, 57.6%) and AI Solutions & Infrastructure ($1,357M, 42.4%), which includes subscriptions, data licensing, and API access. The subscription and API revenue is generated from a much smaller subset of users (117 million Grok MAUs, premium subscribers, enterprise API customers) — not from the full 550 million MAU base. So dividing total AI segment revenue by total MAUs conflates revenue streams that are not generated from the full MAU base.

**2. Disclosure Design (structural reason):**
The 550 million MAU figure is a combined, deduplicated metric across X and Grok as of March 31, 2026 (a point-in-time metric), while the $3,201 million revenue is for the full fiscal year 2025. There's a temporal mismatch — the MAU figure is from Q1 2026, not an average for FY2025. Additionally, the MAU figure includes all X and Grok users, many of whom may not generate any revenue (e.g., free-tier users who don't see ads or subscribe). Meta's DAP, by contrast, is used in conjunction with its ARPP calculation which uses average DAP across quarters. The SpaceX filing doesn't provide an average MAU for FY2025 or a per-user revenue metric analogous to ARPP.

Also, the AI segment revenue includes enterprise API and data licensing revenue that has nothing to do with consumer MAUs at all. These B2B revenue streams are attributed to the AI segment but are not generated from the 550 million consumer MAU base.

Let me also note that the question says "average MAUs" for Meta, but Meta doesn't report MAU. I should note this and use DAP as the closest equivalent.

Let me also check if the SpaceX filing discloses an average MAU for FY2025 or just the point-in-time March 31, 2026 figure.

From the earlier retrieval: "The 550 million figure represents an increase from approximately 520 million MAUs as of December 31, 2025."

So we have:
- December 31, 2025: ~520 million MAU
- March 31, 2026: 550 million MAU

The question specifically asks to use the 550 million figure as of March 31, 2026.

Let me finalize my calculations and analysis.

arguments.prompt: What MAU or monthly active user figures are disclosed for the AI segment or X platform for periods during FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}? Are there average MAU figures for the year, or only point-in-time figures? What was the MAU as of December 31, 2025?

Does the AI segment in {{spacex_s1}} include enterprise API revenue, data licensing revenue, or B2B revenue that is not generated from consumer MAUs? How much of the AI segment revenue comes from non-consumer sources?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the answers to your questions:

*   **Average vs. Point-in-Time Figures:** The document only discloses **point-in-time** MAU figures. The glossary defines MAU as users who have interacted with the platforms "at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement." There are no annual average MAU figures provided for FY2025.
*   **MAU as of December 31, 2025:** As of December 31, 2025, the integrated AI platforms across Grok and X had **approximately 520 million MAUs** (along with over 1.1 billion supported accounts). 

*(Note: The text also mentions that this figure grew to approximately 550 million MAUs as of the March 31, 2026 measurement date).*

Based on the provided text, here is the breakdown regarding the AI segment's non-consumer revenue:

**Does the AI segment include enterprise API, data licensing, and B2B revenue?**
**Yes.** The text explicitly states that the AI segment generates revenue from providing "AI solutions and infrastructure," which includes:
*   **Data licensing arrangements** (recognized ratably as customers consume the licensed data).
*   **API access** to Grok models (recognized ratably for stand-ready access or as services are consumed).
*   **Enterprise B2B offerings**, specifically "tailored deployments of Grok customized to specific workflows and security needs through Grok Business and Grok Enterprise, sold on license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing models."
*   The text also notes a strategic structural advantage as "governments become significant consumers of AI applications."

**How much of the AI segment revenue comes from non-consumer sources?**
The provided text **does not disclose the exact dollar amount or percentage** of AI segment revenue that comes strictly from non-consumer (enterprise, B2B, or government) sources. 

Here is why the exact non-consumer figure cannot be isolated from the text:
1. **Bundled Reporting:** The company reports its AI segment revenue in two main categories: **Advertising** (e.g., $1.84 billion in 2025) and **AI Solutions & Infrastructure** (e.g., $1.35 billion in 2025). 
2. **Mixed Consumer/Non-Consumer Category:** The "AI Solutions & Infrastructure" line item bundles both consumer and non-consumer revenues together. It explicitly includes consumer-facing *"premium subscriptions on X and Grok"* alongside the B2B/enterprise-facing *"data licensing"* and *"API access."*
3. **Historical Context:** The text notes that in 2023 and 2024, "substantially all of our AI segment revenue consisted of advertising, subscriptions, and data licensing revenue generated from X, formerly known as Twitter," suggesting that standalone enterprise API and B2B deployments (like Grok Business/Enterprise) are newer, emerging growth areas rather than historically massive revenue drivers.

**What *is* quantified regarding non-consumer/enterprise growth:**
While the total non-consumer share is not broken out, the text does highlight specific financial movements related to B2B/non-consumer streams:
*   **Data Licensing Growth:** In 2024, the company saw a specific **$90 million increase** in data licensing arrangements.
*   **Overall AI Solutions & Infrastructure Growth:** This combined category (which includes consumer subscriptions, enterprise API, and data licensing) grew from $638 million in 2023 to $892 million in 2024, and to $1,357 million in 2025. 

In summary, while enterprise API, data licensing, and B2B revenues are definitively part of the AI segment's business model and revenue recognition policies, the company does not separate them from consumer subscription revenues in its financial tables, making the exact non-consumer revenue total unavailable in this excerpt.

steps.message: Now I have all the data needed. Let me compile the complete analysis.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile my final answer.

**Meta FY2025:**
- Revenue: $200,966 million
- DAP (December 2025): 3.58 billion
- Note: Meta does not report MAU; it reports DAP (Daily Active People)
- Annual ARPP (disclosed): $57.03
- Calculated revenue per DAP: $200,966M / 3,580M = $56.14

**SpaceX AI Segment FY2025:**
- Revenue: $3,201 million
- MAU (March 31, 2026): 550 million (as specified in the question)
- Calculated revenue per MAU: $3,201M / 550M = $5.82

**Ratio:**
- Using direct calculation: $56.14 / $5.82 ≈ 9.65x
- Using Meta's disclosed ARPP: $57.03 / $5.82 ≈ 9.80x

I'll present both but focus on the direct calculation since the question asks us to "calculate."

**What the gap implies:**
The ~9.6x gap implies that the X-plus-Grok platform is at a much earlier stage of monetization maturity compared to Meta's fully mature social advertising business. Meta has spent over a decade optimizing ad load, targeting, and pricing across its Family of Apps, while the X/Grok platform is still in the early stages of rebuilding its advertising business (which declined from $2.3B in 2023 to $1.8B in 2025) and scaling its subscription and enterprise offerings.

**Two structural reasons:**

1. **Revenue Mix (structural reason):** The AI segment's revenue includes significant non-MAU-driven revenue streams — specifically enterprise API access, data licensing, and B2B/Government deployments (Grok Business/Enterprise) — that are bundled into the "AI Solutions & Infrastructure" line item ($1,357M in FY2025, 42.4% of segment revenue). These B2B revenue streams are not generated from the 550 million consumer MAU base, so dividing total segment revenue by consumer MAUs overstates the per-user monetization of the consumer platform while simultaneously including revenue that has no relationship to the MAU denominator. Meta's revenue, by contrast, is almost entirely advertising-driven and directly tied to its user base.

2. **Disclosure Design (structural reason):** The 550 million MAU figure is a point-in-time metric as of March 31, 2026, while the $3,201 million revenue is for the full fiscal year ended December 31, 2025. This creates a temporal mismatch — the MAU denominator does not represent the average user base during the period when the revenue was generated. The filing does not disclose an average MAU for FY2025 or quarterly MAU averages. By contrast, Meta's ARPP uses average DAP at the beginning and end of each quarter, providing a temporally aligned metric. Additionally, the SpaceX filing does not provide a per-user revenue metric analogous to Meta's ARPP, making it impossible to construct a directly comparable ratio from the filing's own disclosed metrics.

Let me finalize and submit.

arguments.final_result:
## Implied Annual Revenue Per User: Meta vs. SpaceX AI Segment

### 1. Meta Platforms — FY2025 Implied Revenue Per User

**Important note on terminology:** Meta's FY2025 10-K does not disclose Monthly Active Users (MAU). Since approximately 2022, Meta has shifted its primary public reporting metric to **Family Daily Active People (DAP)**, which measures unique individuals across Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp on a given day. DAP is the closest disclosed user-base metric and is used here as the denominator.

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| FY2025 Consolidated Revenue | $200,966 million |
| Family DAP (December 2025 average) | 3.58 billion |
| **Implied Annual Revenue Per DAP** | **$56.14** |
| Meta-disclosed Annual ARPP (FY2025) | $57.03 |

*Calculation: $200,966M ÷ 3,580M = $56.14. The slight difference from Meta's disclosed ARPP of $57.03 arises because ARPP uses average DAP at the beginning and end of each quarter, yielding a slightly lower average denominator (~3.52 billion) than the December 2025 endpoint.*

---

### 2. SpaceX AI Segment — FY2025 Implied Revenue Per MAU

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| FY2025 AI Segment Revenue | $3,201 million |
| Combined X + Grok MAU (March 31, 2026) | 550 million |
| **Implied Annual Revenue Per MAU** | **$5.82** |

*Calculation: $3,201M ÷ 550M = $5.82*

*Note: All historical segment figures reflect the retrospectively recast consolidated entity (including xAI merged February 2, 2026 and X Holdings merged into xAI March 28, 2025). The AI segment revenue of $3,201 million is for the recast FY2025 period.*

---

### 3. Ratio Between the Two Figures

| Comparison | Ratio |
|---|---|
| Meta Revenue/DAP ÷ SpaceX AI Revenue/MAU (direct calculation) | **9.65x** |
| Meta disclosed ARPP ÷ SpaceX AI Revenue/MAU | **9.80x** |

**Meta's implied annual revenue per user is approximately 9.6–9.8x that of SpaceX's AI segment.**

---

### 4. What the Gap Implies About Monetization Maturity

The ~9.7x gap strongly indicates that the X-plus-Grok platform is at a **materially earlier stage of monetization maturity** compared to Meta's fully optimized social advertising ecosystem. Key observations:

- **Meta** has spent over a decade refining ad load optimization, auction-based pricing, behavioral targeting, and impression monetization across four integrated apps (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger). Its ARPP of $57.03 reflects a mature, high-yield advertising machine with 12% YoY ad impression growth and 9% YoY average price-per-ad growth in FY2025.

- **SpaceX's AI segment** is still in a rebuilding and diversification phase. Advertising revenue on X actually *declined* from $2,323M (FY2023) to $1,844M (FY2025), reflecting the platform's ongoing recovery from advertiser attrition. The segment is simultaneously investing heavily in new monetization vectors (Grok subscriptions, API access, enterprise deployments) while running a $(6,355) million operating loss and $(1,237) million Adjusted EBITDA — classic hallmarks of a platform in early-stage monetization build-out.

- The 117 million Grok-specific MAUs (out of 550 million total) suggest that the AI-powered premium monetization layer is still penetrating only ~21% of the user base, leaving substantial room for conversion and ARPU expansion.

---

### 5. Two Structural Reasons This Ratio Cannot Be a Straightforward Monetization Efficiency Comparison

#### Reason 1 — Revenue Mix: B2B/Enterprise Revenue Inflates the Numerator Without Corresponding MAUs

The AI segment's $3,201 million revenue is **not exclusively generated from the 550 million consumer MAU base**. The "AI Solutions & Infrastructure" line item ($1,357 million, or 42.4% of FY2025 AI segment revenue) bundles together:
- Consumer-facing premium subscriptions (X Premium, Grok subscriptions)
- **Enterprise B2B revenue**: data licensing arrangements, Grok API access, and tailored enterprise deployments (Grok Business, Grok Enterprise) sold on license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing models
- **Government AI applications** (referenced as a strategic growth area)

These B2B and government revenue streams are attributed to the AI segment but are generated from enterprise customers and developers — **not from the 550 million consumer MAUs**. Including them in the numerator while using consumer MAUs as the denominator artificially inflates the per-user figure and makes the ratio non-comparable to Meta, where substantially all revenue is advertising-driven and directly tied to user engagement and impressions. By Q1 2026, AI Solutions & Infrastructure had already overtaken advertising as the primary revenue driver (58.1% vs. 41.9%), further widening this structural mismatch.

#### Reason 2 — Disclosure Design: Temporal Mismatch Between Point-in-Time MAU and Full-Year Revenue

The filing's disclosure design creates an **inherent temporal mismatch** that prevents a clean comparison:

- The **550 million MAU figure is a point-in-time snapshot** as of March 31, 2026 (a 30-day measurement period ending on that date). It is not an annual average.
- The **$3,201 million revenue is for the full fiscal year ended December 31, 2025** — a period that ended three months before the MAU measurement date.
- The filing discloses that MAUs were approximately **520 million as of December 31, 2025**, meaning the user base grew ~5.8% between the revenue period's end and the MAU measurement date. Using the higher March 2026 MAU figure as the denominator for FY2025 revenue understates the per-user metric.
- The filing **does not disclose quarterly average MAUs, an annual average MAU, or a per-user revenue metric** analogous to Meta's ARPP (which uses average DAP at the beginning and end of each quarter).

By contrast, Meta's ARPP methodology explicitly aligns its user denominator with the revenue period (quarterly average DAP), enabling a temporally consistent metric. The SpaceX S-1's disclosure design does not provide the data necessary to construct an equivalent temporally aligned ratio, making any comparison inherently imprecise.

---

### Sources

```json

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    },
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1326801/000162828026003942/meta-20251231.htm",
      "name": "Meta Platforms, Inc. FY2025 10-K (filed January 29, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}
```
